Universalist congregations, with their belief in universal salvation and openness to diverse spiritual paths, were natural venues for his message of Vedanta. He drew large audiences from these progressive religious communities.

name: Central Music Hall
significance: Major lecture hall where Vivekananda gave talks during the Parliament
desc: Central Music Hall in Chicago was one of the principal venues where Swami Vivekananda lectured during and after the Parliament of the World's Religions in September 1893. It was a prestigious concert and lecture hall in downtown Chicago, and his appearances there were part of the extension lectures that he gave to the broader public who could not attend the Parliament itself. His talks on Hinduism and Vedanta drew enthusiastic crowds.

name: Auditorium Theater
significance: Prestigious lecture venue in Chicago for Vivekananda
desc: The Auditorium Theatre in Chicago, designed by Adler and Sullivan and one of the most magnificent buildings in America, was a lecture venue associated with Swami Vivekananda's Chicago period. The Parliament extension lectures were hosted at various prestigious venues across the city, and the Auditorium Theatre — with its superb acoustics — was among the most celebrated. Vivekananda's commanding voice and presence were perfectly suited to such grand stages.

name: Cobb Hall
significance: University of Chicago hall where Vivekananda lectured
desc: Cobb Hall at the newly founded University of Chicago was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his Chicago period. The University of Chicago, opened in 1892, was a centre of progressive scholarship, and Vivekananda engaged with its academic community. He presented Vedanta philosophy to American university audiences, arguing for its rational, scientific foundations and its compatibility with modern thought.

name: Dr. Perkins' house
significance: Residence of Chicago supporter Dr. Perkins
desc: Dr. Perkins was among the sympathetic Americans who welcomed Swami Vivekananda into their home during his Chicago sojourn in 1893. Vivekananda was received at many private residences where he held intimate discussions on Vedanta philosophy with small gatherings of interested Americans. These informal parlour talks complemented his public lectures and helped build his early American circle of devotees.

name: First Congretational Church
desc: The First Congregational Church in Evanston, Illinois, was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda spoke during his Illinois tour following the Parliament of the World's Religions. Evanston, home to Northwestern University, provided an educated and culturally receptive audience. Congregational churches, with their tradition of rational and liberal theology, were frequent hosts for his lectures on Vedanta.

name: Houses visited in Evanston
significance: Private homes in Evanston where Vivekananda was hosted
desc: During his visits to Evanston, Illinois, Swami Vivekananda was hosted in the private homes of several sympathetic residents. Evanston, a prosperous university town adjacent to Chicago, had a number of progressive, spiritually inclined families who opened their homes to him. These intimate gatherings in Evanston parlours were some of the earliest forums where Vivekananda introduced Vedanta to American householders.

name: Colonel Ralph Plumb
place: Colonel Ralph Plumb's Residence, Streator, Illinois
significance: Home of Streator supporter Colonel Ralph Plumb
desc: Colonel Ralph Plumb was a civic leader and supporter in Streator, Illinois, who hosted Swami Vivekananda during his lecture tour through the state. Vivekananda visited numerous smaller cities and towns during his American tours, bringing the message of Vedanta to communities beyond the major metropolitan centres. Colonel Plumb's hospitality and introduction helped bring Vivekananda before the audiences of Streator.

name: Plumb Opera House
desc: The Plumb Opera House in Streator, Illinois was the venue for Swami Vivekananda's public lecture in the town. Named after the Plumb family who were prominent in Streator civic life, the opera house served as the principal public gathering place. Vivekananda's lecture on Vedanta and the universal religion drew a curious and enthusiastic audience from this Illinois coal-mining town, demonstrating the wide reach of his American lecture circuit.

name: First Congregational Church
desc: The First Congregational Church in Madison, Wisconsin was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his Midwestern tour. Madison, the state capital and home of the University of Wisconsin, provided a receptive intellectual audience for his message of Vedanta philosophy. Congregational churches across the American Midwest regularly invited him to address their congregations and the wider public.

name: First Unitarian Church
desc: The First Unitarian Church of Minneapolis was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his extensive Midwestern lecture tour. Minneapolis had an active community of liberal religious thinkers, and the Unitarian congregation was among the most receptive to his message of the universality of religion and the philosophical underpinnings of Vedanta. His lectures in Minneapolis drew large and engaged audiences.

name: Quincy House Hotel
significance: Hotel where Vivekananda first stayed in Boston
desc: The Quincy House Hotel in Boston was among the lodgings used by Swami Vivekananda during his Boston visits. Boston was one of his most important centres in America, and he made multiple extended stays in the city between 1893 and 1896. The intellectual and religious openness of Boston — the home of Transcendentalism, Unitarianism and many progressive movements — made it fertile ground for his message of Vedanta.

name: Hotel Bellevue
significance: Hotel used by Vivekananda during Boston stays
desc: Hotel Bellevue in Boston was one of the hotels where Swami Vivekananda lodged during his extended visits to the city. Boston remained one of his principal American bases, and he delivered numerous lectures, classes and private talks from various addresses in the city. The Bellevue was part of the network of accommodations that supported his lecture circuit across New England.

name: Women's Club of Julia Ward Howe
significance: Prominent Boston club where Vivekananda addressed women reformers
desc: The Women's Club associated with Julia Ward Howe — poet, reformer and author of the 'Battle Hymn of the Republic' — was one of the prestigious venues where Swami Vivekananda spoke in Boston. Julia Ward Howe was a towering figure in American progressive life, and her circle included many of the educated, reform-minded women who became some of Vivekananda's most devoted American supporters, including Sara Bull (Mrs. Ole Bull) and others.

name: Round Table
significance: Boston intellectual society that hosted Vivekananda
desc: The Round Table was an intellectual discussion club in Boston that invited Swami Vivekananda to address its members. Boston's rich tradition of intellectual societies and discussion clubs — a legacy of the Transcendentalist movement — provided an ideal forum for his teachings on Vedanta. Members of such clubs were typically drawn from the educated professional class who were open to exploring Indian philosophy.

name: Association Hall
significance: Lecture hall in Boston used by Vivekananda
desc: Association Hall in Boston was one of the public lecture venues used by Swami Vivekananda during his Boston period. He delivered a series of popular lectures here on topics including Hinduism, the philosophy of Vedanta, and the unity of all religions. Boston audiences were among the most intellectually curious he encountered, and Association Hall drew large crowds to hear his talks.

name: Procopeia Club
significance: Boston private club that hosted Vivekananda
desc: The Procopeia Club in Boston was among the private intellectual and social clubs that hosted Swami Vivekananda. Such clubs were a key part of the social infrastructure through which his ideas spread among Boston's educated elite. Members would invite him for intimate lectures and discussions, creating personal connections that formed the backbone of his New England following.

name: Hall at 56 St Botolph St
significance: Boston address associated with Vivekananda's class lectures
desc: The hall at 56 St Botolph Street in Boston was associated with the series of classes and lectures that Swami Vivekananda delivered during his Boston period. The Back Bay neighbourhood, in which St Botolph Street is located, was the heart of Boston's cultivated intellectual and artistic community. Vivekananda held classes here on Raja Yoga, Jnana Yoga and the philosophy of Vedanta for small gatherings of devoted students.

name: Twentieth Century Club
significance: Progressive Boston club that hosted Vivekananda
desc: The Twentieth Century Club in Boston, a forum for progressive discussion on social, literary and philosophical topics, invited Swami Vivekananda to address its members. The club attracted Boston's intellectually progressive community, and Vivekananda's message of Vedanta — with its emphasis on the universality of religion, the divinity of the human soul, and practical ethics — resonated deeply with its membership.

name: Charles Fairchild home
significance: Boston home of devoted supporter Charles Fairchild
desc: Charles Fairchild was one of the devoted Boston supporters of Swami Vivekananda. His home in Boston was a gathering place for Vivekananda's circle of admirers in the city, who included prominent figures from Boston's intellectual and social elite. The Fairchild household was part of the network of private homes that sustained Vivekananda's work in New England and helped spread the message of Vedanta among Boston's educated community.

name: Breezy Meadows
significance: Summer retreat in Metcalf associated with Vivekananda
desc: Breezy Meadows in Metcalf, Massachusetts was a summer retreat where Swami Vivekananda stayed during his New England period. The pleasant countryside around Metcalf provided a welcome respite from the busy lecture circuit, and Vivekananda used such retreats for rest, study and private instruction of his closer disciples. The rural Massachusetts landscape offered a contemplative setting for his ongoing teaching work.

name: Boston Ladies Club
significance: Women's club where Vivekananda lectured in Massachusetts
desc: The Boston Ladies Club was one of the women's clubs in the Boston area that hosted Swami Vivekananda for lectures. Women formed a significant proportion of his American audiences and supporters, attracted by his message of the spiritual equality of all human beings regardless of gender, caste or nationality. His addresses to women's clubs across New England helped build his broad network of supporters.

name: Sherborn Reformatory
place: State Industrial School for Girls, Sherborn, Massachusetts
significance: Massachusetts reformatory where Vivekananda gave a talk
desc: Swami Vivekananda delivered a talk at the State Industrial School (Reformatory) in Sherborn, Massachusetts, in 1894. His willingness to speak at such an institution — reaching out to society's most marginalised — reflected his deep conviction that the divine exists in all human beings, however unfortunate their circumstances. This visit embodied his practical application of the Vedantic principle that service to humanity is service to God.

name: Charlotte Lane Boarding house
significance: Summer boarding house in Annisquam where Vivekananda stayed
desc: The Charlotte Lane Boarding House in Annisquam, the picturesque fishing village within Gloucester on Cape Ann, was a summer residence for Swami Vivekananda during his New England tours. He spent time in Annisquam in 1894 at the invitation of his Boston supporters, and the peaceful seaside village offered an ideal setting for writing, teaching and rest. Several of his American disciples joined him there for instruction.

name: The Hyatt House
significance: Annisquam retreat house associated with Vivekananda
desc: The Hyatt House in Annisquam, Massachusetts, was one of the summer residences associated with Swami Vivekananda's stays in this Cape Ann village. He spent several weeks in Annisquam during the summer of 1894, working on his lectures and classes on Raja Yoga, which were later published as the foundational Vedanta text 'Raja Yoga'. The tranquil coastal setting supported his intensive period of writing and teaching.

name: Universalist Church
significance: Annisquam church where Vivekananda addressed the community
desc: The Universalist Church in Annisquam, Massachusetts was a venue where Swami Vivekananda addressed the local community during his summer visits to this Cape Ann village. Universalist congregations, with their belief in universal salvation and spiritual openness, provided a welcoming platform for his message of Vedanta. Even in the small fishing community of Annisquam, his presence drew curious and receptive audiences.

name: Mechanics Hall
significance: Large public hall in Boston where Vivekananda lectured
desc: Mechanics Hall in Boston was one of the large public lecture venues where Swami Vivekananda addressed audiences during his Boston period. The hall had a long tradition of hosting public lectures, and Vivekananda's talks on Vedanta, Raja Yoga and the science of religion drew sizeable crowds. His Boston lectures were among the most widely attended of his American tour, reflecting the city's keen intellectual interest in Indian philosophy.

name: Kate Tannatt Woods's house
place: Kate Tannatt Woods's House, Salem, Massachusetts
significance: Salem home of author and supporter Kate Tannatt Woods
desc: Kate Tannatt Woods was an American author and journalist who became a supporter of Swami Vivekananda. Her home in Salem, Massachusetts was one of the private residences where he was received during his New England lecture tours. Woods was part of the circle of progressive, literary-minded American women who were drawn to Vivekananda's teachings and helped promote his work in their communities.

name: Wesley Church
desc: Wesley Church in Salem, Massachusetts was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his New England tour. Salem, the historic city associated with the Puritan tradition and the witch trials, had by the late nineteenth century developed a thriving intellectual and reform community. Methodist congregations like Wesley Church occasionally opened their pulpits to Vivekananda as interest in comparative religion grew across American denominations.

name: East Church
significance: Salem church venue for Vivekananda lectures
desc: The East Church in Salem, Massachusetts was among the historic Congregational churches in this ancient New England town that hosted Swami Vivekananda for a lecture during his New England tour. Salem's long tradition of religious inquiry — even if turbulent in its early history — had evolved by Vivekananda's time into a thoughtful openness to comparative religion, and he found receptive audiences here.

name: Northampton City Hall
significance: Civic venue in Northampton where Vivekananda lectured
desc: Northampton City Hall in western Massachusetts was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his New England tour. Northampton, a prosperous town in the Connecticut River valley and home to Smith College, provided a cultivated audience for his talks on Vedanta and the universal religion. His willingness to lecture in civic halls as well as churches reflected his democratic approach to sharing the message of Vedanta.

name: Smith College
desc: Smith College in Northampton, Massachusetts — one of the leading women's colleges in America — was a venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his New England tour. His message of the spiritual equality of women and the recognition of the divine in all human beings was naturally welcomed at a women's institution. Educated American women formed a crucial constituency among his earliest American devotees.

name: Oxford Club lecture hall
desc: The Oxford Club lecture hall in Lynn, Massachusetts was a venue where Swami Vivekananda addressed a local audience during his New England circuit. Lynn, the historic shoe-manufacturing city north of Boston, had a community of progressive thinkers who invited him to speak. His lecture on Vedanta philosophy and the universal principles of religion drew an engaged audience from this working-class Massachusetts city.

name: Rockland Street
significance: Swampscott address associated with Vivekananda's stay
desc: Rockland Street in Swampscott, Massachusetts was associated with Swami Vivekananda's visits to this seaside town north of Boston. Swampscott, a pleasant resort community on the Atlantic shore, attracted Boston's educated middle class in summer, and several of Vivekananda's supporters had residences here. He visited Swampscott during his New England tours and held informal gatherings and talks for interested residents.

name: Library Hall
desc: Library Hall in Lawrence, Massachusetts was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his New England tour. Lawrence, the textile-manufacturing city on the Merrimack River, had a diverse immigrant population and a tradition of public lectures. Vivekananda's appearances in industrial cities like Lawrence demonstrated his commitment to bringing the message of Vedanta to all segments of American society, not merely the educated elite.

name: Rogers Hall
significance: Melrose lecture hall used by Vivekananda
desc: Rogers Hall in Melrose, Massachusetts was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his tour of the towns north of Boston. Melrose, a prosperous residential community, had an active cultural and lecture-going community. Vivekananda's talks in such suburban venues helped spread the message of Vedanta beyond the major cities and into the comfortable New England communities that formed the backbone of his American support.

name: Evans Caswell's house
significance: Melrose private home where Vivekananda was hosted
desc: Evans Caswell's home in Melrose, Massachusetts was one of the private residences where Swami Vivekananda was received during his New England tours. The pattern of visits to private homes for intimate discussions and talks was central to his method of teaching in America, where the personal connection established in a drawing-room setting often proved more transformative than public lectures.

name: Magnolia Library
significance: Library in Magnolia where Vivekananda spoke
desc: The Magnolia Library in Magnolia, Massachusetts — a fashionable summer colony within Gloucester on Cape Ann — was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his New England visits. Libraries and reading rooms were important community gathering places in Victorian America, and Magnolia's summer residents, drawn from Boston's professional class, provided an educated and receptive audience for his talks on Vedanta philosophy.

name: Gray Beach
significance: Coastal location in Magnolia associated with Vivekananda
desc: Gray Beach in Magnolia, Massachusetts was associated with Swami Vivekananda's summer stays in this Cape Ann resort village. The rocky Atlantic coastline of Magnolia, with its bracing sea air and dramatic scenery, provided a contemplative backdrop for his work. Like the rocks at Kanyakumari where he had his famous meditation in 1892, the seashore held a special significance for him as a place of spiritual reflection.

name: Davis Opera House
desc: The Davis Opera House in Plymouth, Massachusetts, the historic 'Plymouth Rock' town that was the landing place of the Pilgrims, was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his New England tour. The irony was not lost on him that he was bringing the ancient wisdom of India to the very town most associated with the Puritan founding of America. He saw in such encounters a fulfilment of the destiny of East and West to meet and enrich each other.

name: Radcliffe College(Fay House)
place: Radcliffe College, Fay House, Cambridge, Massachusetts
significance: Women's college at Harvard where Vivekananda lectured
desc: Radcliffe College (now Radcliffe Institute for Advanced Study at Harvard), with its historic Fay House, was a venue where Swami Vivekananda addressed students and faculty during his Cambridge period. As the coordinate women's college affiliated with Harvard, Radcliffe attracted the most intellectually ambitious women in America. Vivekananda's series of lectures in Cambridge in 1896 were among the most academically rigorous he delivered in America.

name: Sever Hall
place: Sever Hall, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts
desc: Sever Hall at Harvard University was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his Cambridge period in 1896. Designed by H.H. Richardson, Sever Hall was one of Harvard's principal lecture and classroom buildings. Vivekananda's lectures at Harvard — before one of the world's most distinguished academic audiences — represented a high point in his American mission, as he presented Vedanta as a rigorous philosophical system worthy of the highest intellectual engagement.

name: Sara Ole Bull House
place: Sara Ole Bull's House, Cambridge, Massachusetts
significance: Cambridge home of devotee Sara Bull who was Vivekananda's principal American patron
desc: The Cambridge home of Sara Chapman Bull (Mrs. Ole Bull), widow of the Norwegian violinist Ole Bull, was one of Swami Vivekananda's most important American addresses. Sara Bull was his most devoted and generous American patron, providing him with financial support and a welcoming home during his Boston and Cambridge periods. She later funded the founding of Vedanta Society centres and continued to support his work until her death. Her elegant Cambridge home was a hub of Vivekananda's New England circle.

name: Mrs. Anna Richards House
place: Mrs. Anna Richards' House, Cambridge, Massachusetts
significance: Cambridge home of devotee Mrs. Anna Richards
desc: Mrs. Anna Richards' home in Cambridge, Massachusetts was one of the private residences associated with Swami Vivekananda's Cambridge circle. Anna Richards was among the devoted women supporters who formed the core of his New England following. Her home provided a welcoming space for the intimate gatherings and discussions that complemented his public lectures and helped deepen the commitment of his closest American disciples.

name: Dane Hall
place: Dane Hall, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts
significance: Harvard hall associated with Vivekananda's Cambridge lectures
desc: Dane Hall at Harvard University was associated with Swami Vivekananda's lectures and meetings during his Cambridge period. Harvard's academic buildings provided the setting for some of his most intellectual engagements in America. His lectures in Cambridge were later reported in newspapers and drew the attention of distinguished scholars including William James and other leaders of American academic life.

name: Prof.J.H Wright's house
place: Prof. John Henry Wright's House, Cambridge, Massachusetts
significance: Cambridge home of Prof. John Henry Wright who helped Vivekananda attend the Parliament
desc: Professor John Henry Wright of Harvard University played a pivotal role in Swami Vivekananda's American career. When Vivekananda arrived in Boston in 1893 without credentials to attend the Parliament of the World's Religions, Prof. Wright — a Greek scholar who was deeply impressed by his knowledge — wrote him a letter of introduction, famously saying: 'To ask for your credentials is like asking the sun to state its right to shine.' This letter secured Vivekananda's place at the Parliament. Wright's Cambridge home was where Vivekananda stayed before travelling to Chicago.

name: Memorial Hall
place: Memorial Hall, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts
significance: Harvard's great hall where Vivekananda lectured
desc: Memorial Hall at Harvard University — the great Victorian Gothic building erected in memory of Harvard's Civil War dead — was a prominent lecture venue associated with Swami Vivekananda's Cambridge period. His lectures at Harvard in the spring of 1896, attended by faculty, students and Cambridge society, were a landmark in his American mission. The Harvard lectures demonstrated that Vedanta philosophy could hold its own in the most demanding academic environment in America.

name: Holworthy Hall
place: Holworthy Hall, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts
significance: Harvard dormitory associated with Vivekananda's Cambridge visit
desc: Holworthy Hall, one of the oldest dormitories at Harvard University, was associated with Swami Vivekananda's visits to the Harvard campus during his Cambridge period in 1896. The ancient Harvard Yard, with its historic brick buildings, provided the atmospheric setting for his lectures and meetings with American scholars and students who were deeply curious about Indian philosophy and the Vedantic tradition.

name: Prof. James' house
place: Prof. William James' House, Cambridge, Massachusetts
significance: Home of philosopher William James who met Vivekananda
desc: The Cambridge home of Professor William James — the eminent philosopher and psychologist and founder of pragmatism — was where Swami Vivekananda met one of America's greatest thinkers. James and Vivekananda had a profound mutual respect. James, who had written 'The Varieties of Religious Experience,' was deeply interested in the psychology of mysticism and found in Vivekananda a living embodiment of the mystical consciousness he had theorised. James reportedly said of Vivekananda: 'He is a man who is above everything.' Their exchanges in Cambridge enriched both thinkers.

name: MacLeod's house
significance: Cambridge home of devotee Josephine MacLeod
desc: The Cambridge home of Josephine MacLeod (affectionately called 'Joe Joe' by Vivekananda) was one of his important addresses during his New England period. Josephine MacLeod was one of his most devoted American disciples, who later followed him to India and remained a lifelong friend and supporter. Her home in the Cambridge area was a gathering place for his inner circle of American devotees during his Cambridge lecture series of 1896.

name: Grand Army Hall
significance: Medford civic hall where Vivekananda lectured
desc: The Grand Army Hall in Medford, Massachusetts — named after the Grand Army of the Republic, the Civil War veterans' organisation — was a civic lecture venue where Swami Vivekananda addressed a local audience. These community halls in the towns north of Boston provided accessible public forums for his message of Vedanta, reaching audiences beyond the metropolitan centres and the private clubs of the intellectual elite.

name: Mrs. Emily Ruth Gibson's house
place: Mrs. Emily Ruth Gibson's House, Medford, Massachusetts
significance: Medford home of supporter Mrs. Emily Ruth Gibson
desc: Mrs. Emily Ruth Gibson's home in Medford, Massachusetts was one of the private residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his New England lecture circuit. Mrs. Gibson was part of the network of progressive, spiritually interested women supporters who organised his visits to communities north of Boston. Her home was likely the setting for an intimate lecture or discussion session for a small circle of interested Medford residents.

name: Sanatorium
significance: Saratoga Springs health resort visited by Vivekananda
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited a sanatorium in Saratoga Springs, the famous spa and resort town in upstate New York. Saratoga Springs, known for its natural mineral springs and fashionable social scene, attracted America's wealthy elite. Vivekananda visited the town during his New York period, meeting with interested supporters and lecturing to the summer society that gathered here. The mineral springs and health-seeking atmosphere of Saratoga resonated with his interest in the relationship between physical and spiritual wellbeing.

name: Town Hall
place: Town Hall, Saratoga Springs, New York
significance: Saratoga Springs civic venue for Vivekananda lecture
desc: The Town Hall in Saratoga Springs, New York was a lecture venue for Swami Vivekananda during his visits to this famous upstate New York spa resort. Saratoga Springs attracted summer visitors from New York City's wealthy and intellectual classes, and the Town Hall provided a dignified civic setting for his public lectures on Vedanta, the universal religion and the philosophies of ancient India.

name: Fishkill Landing - Beacon
significance: Hudson River town where Vivekananda visited supporters
desc: Fishkill Landing (renamed Beacon in 1913) is a town on the eastern shore of the Hudson River in Dutchess County, New York. Swami Vivekananda visited this Hudson Valley community during his New York period, meeting with local supporters. The picturesque Hudson River valley, with its dramatic landscapes, was a region he traversed in his visits to various New York communities outside the city.

name: Dr. Guernsey's home
place: Dr. Guernsey's Home, New York City
significance: New York home of early supporter Dr. Guernsey
desc: Dr. Guernsey was one of Swami Vivekananda's early New York supporters who hosted him at his home during the initial period of his American mission. New York City became his principal American base, and a network of sympathetic hosts — particularly from the medical and professional class — helped sustain his work there. Dr. Guernsey's home was one of the early gathering places for his New York circle of devotees.

name: 54 West 33rd St (First HQ)
significance: First headquarters of Vedanta work in New York
desc: 54 West 33rd Street in Manhattan was the first headquarters for Swami Vivekananda's organised Vedanta work in New York City. It was here that he established the first classes and meetings that would eventually evolve into the Vedanta Society of New York. From this address he conducted regular lectures and classes on Raja Yoga, Jnana Yoga and Bhakti Yoga for his growing circle of New York students, marking the formal beginning of the institutionalised Vedanta movement in America.

name: Waldorf Hotel
place: Waldorf Hotel, Fifth Avenue, New York City
significance: Prestigious Manhattan hotel where Vivekananda stayed and met supporters
desc: The original Waldorf Hotel on Fifth Avenue (later part of the Waldorf-Astoria) was among the prestigious Manhattan hotels where Swami Vivekananda stayed and met supporters during his New York period. The Waldorf was one of the most fashionable addresses in America, and Vivekananda's presence there reflected the high social circles in which he moved in New York. Wealthy patrons and society figures who supported his work would often arrange meetings at such establishments.

name: Mary Phillips's Home
place: Mary Phillips's Home, New York City
significance: New York home of devoted supporter Mary Phillips
desc: Mary Phillips was one of the devoted New York supporters of Swami Vivekananda who opened her home to him and his circle. Her home in Manhattan was a gathering place for his New York devotees, hosting the private talks and informal discussions that complemented his public lectures. Women like Mary Phillips formed the inner circle of practical supporters who managed the logistics of his American work and provided the domestic base from which he operated.

name: Unknown place in New York
significance: Unidentified New York location associated with Vivekananda
desc: This pin marks a New York City location associated with Swami Vivekananda's time in the city whose precise address has not been definitively established in historical records. During his extended New York periods — particularly 1894-1895 and 1899-1900 — he visited numerous addresses across Manhattan, delivering lectures, holding private classes and meeting supporters. Some of these venues and residences have not been fully documented in the historical record.

name: Leon Landsberg's Home
place: Leon Landsberg's Home, New York City
significance: New York home of early disciple Leon Landsberg
desc: Leon Landsberg (Swami Kripananda) was one of Swami Vivekananda's first American monastic disciples. A Jewish American journalist and intellectual, Landsberg was drawn to Vivekananda's teachings on Vedanta and became one of his earliest initiated disciples in America. His home in New York City was one of the gathering places for Vivekananda's early New York circle, and he played an important role in the early organisation of Vedanta work in the city.

name: Emma Thursby's house
place: Emma Thursby's House, New York City
significance: Home of celebrated soprano Emma Thursby who supported Vivekananda
desc: Emma Cecilia Thursby was one of America's most celebrated soprano singers of the nineteenth century, and she became a devoted supporter of Swami Vivekananda. Her New York home was an important social venue where he met members of New York's cultural and artistic elite. Thursby's support lent Vivekananda considerable social prestige in New York, and her circle included many of the city's most distinguished artistic and intellectual figures.

name: Corbin mansion
significance: New York mansion of supporter associated with Vivekananda
desc: The Corbin mansion in New York City was one of the elegant residences associated with Swami Vivekananda's New York social circle. New York's wealthy elite — including figures from the finance and business world — were among the supporters who attended his lectures and hosted him at their homes. The parlours of such mansions provided settings for the more intimate and exclusive gatherings that complemented his large public lectures.

name: Home of Lorenzo Barbers
place: Home of Lorenzo Barbers, New York City
significance: New York home of supporter Lorenzo Barbers
desc: The home of Lorenzo Barbers in New York City was one of the private residences where Swami Vivekananda was received during his New York period. Like the private homes of supporters across America, Barbers' residence provided a domestic space for the intimate discussions on Vedanta that formed a crucial complement to his public lectures. These personal connections with individual American families helped root his teachings in the daily lives of his earliest American disciples.

name: Hall of Universal Brotherhood
place: Hall of Universal Brotherhood, New York City
significance: New York hall associated with Vivekananda's lectures
desc: The Hall of Universal Brotherhood in New York City was a lecture venue associated with Swami Vivekananda's New York work. The name of the hall resonated with his core teaching of the unity of all existence and the brotherhood of all human beings, which was a central theme of his American mission. He used such venues for public lectures on Vedanta, Raja Yoga and the universal principles of spiritual life that transcend all sectarian boundaries.

name: Exodus club
significance: New York club where Vivekananda lectured
desc: The Exodus Club in New York City was one of the private clubs and societies where Swami Vivekananda was invited to speak during his New York period. Such clubs provided an intimate setting for his talks on Vedanta philosophy and the universal religion, reaching an educated audience of New York professionals and intellectuals. The variety of venues he addressed — from fashionable clubs to working-class lecture halls — reflected the breadth of his American appeal.

name: Mott Memorial Hall
desc: Swami Vivekananda delivered public lectures at Mott Memorial Hall in New York City during his first visit to America (1894-1895), drawing large audiences eager to hear his teachings on Vedanta and Indian philosophy.

name: 228 West Thirty-ninth Street(Second HQ)
desc: 228 West Thirty-ninth Street served as the second headquarters of Vivekananda's Vedanta work in New York City, where he organized classes and meetings after relocating from his earlier base during his first Western tour.

name: Hardman Hall
desc: Hardman Hall in New York City was among the prominent venues where Swami Vivekananda held public lectures and classes on Vedanta, attracting intellectuals and spiritual seekers from across the city.

name: Madison Square Garden Concert Hall
desc: Swami Vivekananda spoke at the Madison Square Garden Concert Hall in New York City, one of the prestigious venues of the era, drawing large crowds to hear his inspiring discourses on Vedanta, universal religion, and Indian civilization.

name: Ramakrishna-Vivekananda Center of New York
significance: Vedanta center founded in Vivekananda's legacy
desc: The Ramakrishna-Vivekananda Center of New York traces its spiritual lineage directly to Swami Vivekananda's pioneering Vedanta work in New York. Vivekananda's efforts during 1894-1895 laid the foundation for this enduring centre of Vedantic study and practice in New York City.

name: Charles Higgins Home
desc: The home of Charles Higgins in Brooklyn was one of the residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his time in the New York area, reflecting the warm hospitality he received from American devotees and admirers during his first Western tour.

name: Pouch Mansion
significance: Event venue associated with Vivekananda's visit
desc: Pouch Mansion was a notable venue in the New York area associated with events during Swami Vivekananda's first Western tour, where he interacted with American society and furthered his mission of spreading Vedanta in the West.

name: Charles Auel's house
desc: The home of Charles Auel in Brooklyn was one of the residences where Swami Vivekananda stayed or visited during his time in the New York metropolitan area, part of the network of supportive American hosts during his first Western tour.

name: The Long Island Historical Society
significance: Cultural institution visited during Brooklyn stay
desc: The Long Island Historical Society (now the Brooklyn Historical Society) in Brooklyn Heights is associated with Swami Vivekananda's engagement with New York cultural institutions during his first Western tour in 1895.

name: Robertson Hall
significance: Lecture or meeting venue in Brooklyn
desc: Robertson Hall in Brooklyn was a venue where Swami Vivekananda spoke or conducted meetings during his time in the New York area, part of his extensive lecture circuit during the first Western tour of 1893-1895.

name: Sarah Ellen Waldo's house
significance: Home of a devoted follower and host
desc: Sarah Ellen Waldo was an early American devotee and supporter of Swami Vivekananda in New York. Her home served as a place of Vedantic discussion and spiritual fellowship during Vivekananda's first Western tour, and she contributed to spreading his teachings among New York society.

name: Ridgely Manor
significance: Country retreat visited during first Western tour
desc: Ridgely Manor in Stone Ridge, New York, was the country estate where Swami Vivekananda rested and engaged in spiritual discussions with American devotees during the summer of 1895. These peaceful retreats in the Hudson Valley allowed him time for reflection between his busy lecture engagements.

name: Lyndhurst
significance: Hudson Valley estate visited during first Western tour
desc: Lyndhurst, the historic Gothic Revival estate in Irvington along the Hudson River, is connected with Swami Vivekananda's travels through the Hudson Valley area during his first Western tour in 1895, part of his engagements with prominent American families.

name: Central Church of Christ
desc: Swami Vivekananda delivered lectures at the Central Church of Christ in Des Moines, Iowa, during his first Western tour in 1894. His message of universal religion and Vedanta resonated with American audiences, including those gathered at this prominent religious venue.

name: Rev. Breeden's home
significance: Hosted Vivekananda during Des Moines visit
desc: The home of Reverend Breeden in Des Moines, Iowa, was one of the residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his visit to Des Moines in 1894, reflecting the openness of American clergy to engage with his teachings on universal religion.

name: Savery Hotel
significance: Hotel where Vivekananda stayed in Des Moines
desc: The Savery Hotel in Des Moines, Iowa, was where Swami Vivekananda stayed during his visit to the city in 1894. The historic hotel served as his base while he gave lectures and met with admirers in Des Moines during his first Western tour.

name: Drake University
significance: University lecture venue in Des Moines
desc: Swami Vivekananda lectured at Drake University in Des Moines, Iowa, during his first Western tour in 1894. His talks on Vedanta and Indian philosophy were well-received by the academic community, demonstrating his ability to engage with educated American audiences.

name: La Salette Academy
desc: La Salette Academy in Memphis, Tennessee, was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda spoke during his visit to Memphis in 1894. His lectures on Vedanta and universal brotherhood drew audiences from the city's educated and religious circles.

name: The Tennessee Club
significance: Social venue where Vivekananda was received
desc: The Tennessee Club in Memphis, a prestigious social institution, was among the venues where Swami Vivekananda was received and gave talks during his visit to Memphis, Tennessee in 1894, demonstrating his acceptance by Southern American society.

name: Randolph Building
desc: The Randolph Building in Memphis, Tennessee, was a venue associated with Swami Vivekananda's lecture engagements in Memphis during his first Western tour in 1894, as he spread the message of Vedanta across the American South.

name: Auditorium
significance: Major public lecture venue in Memphis
desc: The Auditorium in Memphis, Tennessee, was one of the major public venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered lectures during his visit to Memphis in 1894, attracting large audiences drawn by his eloquence and the novelty of his Vedantic teachings.

name: The Woman's Council
desc: The Woman's Council of Memphis, Tennessee, hosted Swami Vivekananda during his 1894 visit to the city. American women's organizations were among his most enthusiastic supporters, and Vivekananda frequently addressed such groups with his empowering message of spiritual equality.

name: Young Men's Hebrew Association Hall
desc: The Young Men's Hebrew Association Hall in Memphis, Tennessee, was one of the diverse venues where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1894 visit, reflecting his universal appeal across different religious and cultural communities in America.

name: Mrs.Bagley residence
desc: The residence of Mrs. Bagley in Detroit, Michigan, was among the homes where Swami Vivekananda was graciously hosted during his visit to Detroit in 1894. Such private residences provided him with rest and intimate spiritual conversations with American devotees during his first Western tour.

name: Detroit club
significance: Social club where Vivekananda spoke in Detroit
desc: The Detroit Club, one of the city's premier social institutions, hosted Swami Vivekananda during his visit to Detroit in 1894. His talks at such prestigious venues reached Detroit's civic and business leaders, furthering the spread of Vedantic ideas among influential American circles.

name: First Congregational Unitarian Church
significance: Church where Vivekananda lectured in Detroit
desc: Swami Vivekananda delivered lectures at the First Congregational Unitarian Church in Detroit, Michigan, in 1894. Unitarian congregations across America were particularly receptive to his message of universal religion and the harmony of all faiths, making them frequent hosts during his first Western tour.

name: T W Palmer House
significance: Home of Senator T. W. Palmer, hosted Vivekananda
desc: The home of Senator Thomas W. Palmer in Detroit was one of the distinguished residences where Swami Vivekananda was received during his 1894 visit. Senator Palmer's hospitality reflected the high social standing Vivekananda had achieved in American society following his triumph at the Parliament of Religions.

name: Richelieu Hotel
significance: Hotel where Vivekananda stayed in Detroit
desc: The Richelieu Hotel in Detroit, Michigan, served as Swami Vivekananda's base during his visit to the city in 1894. From here he ventured out for his various lecture engagements, spreading the message of Vedanta across the Detroit metropolitan area during his first Western tour.

name: Temple Beth El
desc: Swami Vivekananda lectured at Temple Beth El in Detroit, Michigan, in 1894. His willingness to speak at a Jewish house of worship exemplified his deep belief in the universality of religion — that all paths lead to the same divine truth — and earned him admiration across Detroit's diverse religious communities.

name: Detroit Opera House
significance: Major public lecture venue in Detroit
desc: The Detroit Opera House was among the grandest venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered public lectures during his 1894 visit to Detroit. The prestige of this venue underscored the remarkable reception he received in America, attracting large audiences eager to hear his eloquent discourses on Vedanta and Indian spirituality.

name: Detroit Auditorium
significance: Large public lecture hall in Detroit
desc: The Detroit Auditorium hosted Swami Vivekananda's public lectures during his visit to Detroit, Michigan, in 1894. As one of the city's largest gathering places, it allowed him to address a broad cross-section of Detroit's population with his inspiring message of Vedanta and the universal brotherhood of all humanity.

name: Bay City Opera House
significance: Lecture venue in Bay City, Michigan
desc: Swami Vivekananda lectured at the Bay City Opera House in Bay City, Michigan, during his 1894 tour of the American Midwest. Even in smaller cities like Bay City, his reputation ensured enthusiastic audiences drawn to his talks on the universality of religion and the wisdom of India's Vedantic tradition.

name: Academy of Music
desc: The Academy of Music in Saginaw, Michigan, was the venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1894 visit to the city. His tour of Michigan cities including Saginaw brought his teachings on Vedanta and the harmony of world religions to audiences throughout the Great Lakes region.

name: Ada Opera house
desc: Swami Vivekananda spoke at the Ada Opera House in Ada, Ohio, during his first Western tour in 1894. His willingness to travel to smaller towns across America demonstrated his determination to bring the message of Vedanta to all segments of American society, not just the major urban centres.

name: Memorial Opera House
desc: The Memorial Opera House in Valparaiso, Indiana, hosted Swami Vivekananda during his lecture tour of the American Midwest in 1894. Valparaiso, home to a prominent university, provided an intellectually receptive audience for his discourses on Vedanta philosophy and the spiritual heritage of India.

name: Greenacre Inn Conference
desc: The Greenacre Inn Conference in Eliot, Maine, was an important venue of spiritual exchange where Swami Vivekananda participated during his first Western tour. Greenacre, founded by Sarah Farmer, was a pioneering interfaith gathering place that attracted spiritual thinkers from East and West, and Vivekananda's presence there deepened America's engagement with Indian philosophy.

name: Lysekloster Pines
significance: Retreat grounds near Greenacre, Eliot, Maine
desc: Lysekloster Pines in Eliot, Maine, was part of the Greenacre retreat grounds where Swami Vivekananda spent time during the summer spiritual conferences. The tranquil pine-shaded setting provided an ideal environment for meditation, discourse, and the informal exchange of spiritual ideas with American seekers.

name: Vrooman brothers residence
desc: The residence of the Vrooman brothers in Baltimore, Maryland, was among the homes where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his visit to the city in 1894. The Vrooman family were part of the progressive American intellectual circles that embraced Vivekananda's message of Vedanta and universal religion.

name: Hotel Rennert
significance: Hotel where Vivekananda stayed in Baltimore
desc: The Hotel Rennert in Baltimore, Maryland, was one of the finest hotels in the city, and served as Swami Vivekananda's base during his visit to Baltimore in 1894. From here he conducted his lecture engagements and met with the many American admirers who came to discuss Vedanta and Indian philosophy with him.

name: Lyceum Theater
desc: The Lyceum Theater in Baltimore, Maryland, was a venue where Swami Vivekananda delivered public lectures during his 1894 visit to the city. Baltimore's Lyceum was one of many American theatres and halls where his powerful oratory on Vedanta, Indian civilization, and the unity of all religions captivated large audiences.

name: Geo Grafflin's House
significance: Private residence hosting Vivekananda in Baltimore
desc: The home of George Grafflin in Baltimore, Maryland, was among the private residences where Swami Vivekananda was welcomed during his 1894 visit to the city. Such gatherings in private homes allowed for intimate discussions of Vedantic philosophy with Baltimore's educated and spiritually curious residents.

name: Harris' Academy of Music Concert Hall
place: Harris' Academy of Music Concert Hall
significance: Major concert hall lecture venue in Baltimore
desc: Harris' Academy of Music Concert Hall in Baltimore, Maryland, was one of the premier venues where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1894 visit. The hall's reputation for hosting distinguished speakers and performers ensured a large and appreciative audience for his talks on the universal principles of Vedanta and the spiritual wisdom of India.

name: Typographical Temple(People's Church)
desc: Swami Vivekananda lectured at the Typographical Temple, also known as the People's Church, in Washington D.C. during his first Western tour in 1894. The venue's association with working people and progressive causes resonated with his message of practical Vedanta and the spiritual dignity of all human beings.

name: Mrs.E.Totten residence
desc: The residence of Mrs. E. Totten in Washington D.C. was one of the distinguished homes where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his 1894 visit to the capital. Such private gatherings in the homes of influential Washington residents allowed for intimate discussions of Vedantic philosophy with America's political and social elite.

name: Metzerott Music Hall
significance: Major lecture venue in Washington D.C.
desc: Metzerott Music Hall in Washington D.C. was one of the premier public venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered lectures during his 1894 visit to the nation's capital. The hall's reputation for hosting distinguished performers and speakers ensured a large audience drawn by curiosity about this remarkable Indian monk's teachings on Vedanta.

name: Unity Hall
desc: Unity Hall in Hartford, Connecticut, was the venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his visit to the city in 1895. Hartford's progressive intellectual culture made it a receptive host for his talks on Vedanta, and Unity Hall provided a fitting setting for his message of the unity underlying all religious traditions.

name: White Birch Lodge
significance: Retreat lodge in the White Mountains, New Hampshire
desc: White Birch Lodge in Percy, New Hampshire, was a retreat destination in the scenic White Mountains where Swami Vivekananda rested during the summer of 1895. The natural beauty and quietude of the New Hampshire mountains provided him with much-needed respite between his intensive lecture engagements during the first Western tour.

name: Stimpson Mansion
desc: The Stimpson Mansion in Los Angeles was among the grand residences where Swami Vivekananda was received during his 1900 visit to Southern California. His second Western tour brought him to California's growing cities, where he attracted devoted followers among the spiritually-minded residents of Los Angeles.

name: Roxie Blodgett Cottage
significance: Home of a devoted follower in Los Angeles
desc: The cottage of Roxie Blodgett in Los Angeles was a place where Swami Vivekananda was welcomed and hosted during his 1900 California visit. Such devoted American followers provided him with comfortable stays and helped organise his lecture engagements in Southern California during his second Western tour.

name: Blanchard Hall
desc: Blanchard Hall in Los Angeles was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered public lectures during his 1900 visit to Southern California. The hall provided a public platform for his talks on Vedanta, Raja Yoga, and the spiritual traditions of India to Los Angeles audiences.

name: Unity Church
desc: Swami Vivekananda spoke at the Unity Church in Los Angeles during his 1900 California visit. The Unity movement's emphasis on the practical application of spiritual principles aligned naturally with Vivekananda's own teachings on practical Vedanta, making this an especially receptive audience for his message.

name: Payne's Hall
desc: Payne's Hall in Los Angeles was a venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1900 visit to Southern California. Along with other lecture halls and private residences, it formed part of the circuit through which he spread the teachings of Vedanta among Los Angeles audiences during his second Western tour.

name: Home of Truth
significance: New Thought spiritual centre in Los Angeles
desc: The Home of Truth in Los Angeles was a New Thought spiritual centre associated with Swami Vivekananda's activities during his 1900 California visit. The Home of Truth movement, which emphasised spiritual healing and the power of mind, intersected with Vivekananda's Vedantic teachings, attracting seekers from across Los Angeles.

name: Echo Mountain House
desc: Echo Mountain House, situated in the San Gabriel Mountains above Pasadena, was a destination Swami Vivekananda visited during his 1900 California stay. The mountain setting offered him a tranquil retreat from the busy lecture circuit, and the panoramic views of Southern California provided a fitting backdrop for contemplation.

name: Mount Lowe
significance: Mountain excursion site near Los Angeles
desc: Mount Lowe in the San Gabriel Mountains was among the natural landmarks visited by Swami Vivekananda during his stay in the Los Angeles area in 1900. The Mount Lowe Railway was one of the great tourist attractions of California at the time, and Vivekananda's excursion there offered him an experience of the grand California landscape.

name: Mrs.Newton's house
desc: The home of Mrs. Newton in South Pasadena, California, was one of the residences where Swami Vivekananda stayed during his 1900 visit to the Pasadena area. The leafy, pleasant suburb provided a restful setting between his lecture engagements in Los Angeles during his second Western tour.

name: Mrs.E.F.Bowler house
desc: The home of Mrs. E. F. Bowler in South Pasadena, California, was among the private residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his 1900 visit to Southern California. These warm-hearted American hostesses played an important role in supporting his mission by providing comfortable accommodation and organising gatherings of spiritual seekers.

name: Green Hotel
desc: The Green Hotel in the Pasadena area of Southern California was associated with Swami Vivekananda's stay during his 1900 California visit. Pasadena's elegant hotels and cultural venues provided a congenial setting for this period of his second Western tour.

name: Shakespeare club
significance: Literary club where Vivekananda spoke in South Pasadena
desc: The Shakespeare Club in South Pasadena, California, hosted Swami Vivekananda during his 1900 California visit. Such literary and cultural clubs were frequently the venues for his talks on Indian philosophy and Vedanta, as their educated, culturally-minded memberships proved receptive audiences for his eloquent discourses.

name: Universalist Church
desc: The Universalist Church in South Pasadena, California, was a venue where Swami Vivekananda spoke during his 1900 California visit. Universalist congregations, with their belief in the salvation of all souls and the harmony of religions, were naturally sympathetic to Vivekananda's Vedantic message of universal spirituality.

name: Meads Sisters house
desc: The home of the Meads Sisters in South Pasadena, California, was one of the welcoming residences where Swami Vivekananda was received during his 1900 stay in the Pasadena area. The sisters were among his devoted American followers in Southern California who helped spread his teachings on Vedanta.

name: First Unitarian Church
desc: Swami Vivekananda lectured at the First Unitarian Church in Oakland, California, during his 1900 West Coast visit. Oakland's Unitarian congregation, known for its progressive and intellectually open approach to religion, provided an ideal audience for his teachings on the universality of Vedanta and the common spiritual core of all religions.

name: George H.Perry house
desc: The home of George H. Perry in Alameda, California, was one of the Bay Area residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his 1900 California visit. Alameda, situated on an island in San Francisco Bay, was home to several of his devoted California followers who welcomed him warmly during his second Western tour.

name: Alameda Home of Truth
significance: New Thought spiritual centre in Alameda
desc: The Alameda Home of Truth was a New Thought spiritual centre in Alameda, California, associated with Swami Vivekananda's Bay Area activities in 1900. The Home of Truth network across California provided organised venues and receptive audiences for his teachings on Vedanta and the practical application of spiritual principles.

name: Tucker Hall
desc: Tucker Hall in Alameda, California, was a venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1900 Bay Area visit. As part of his extensive California lecture circuit, Tucker Hall allowed him to bring his message of Vedanta to the residents of this Bay Area community during his second and final Western tour.

name: Home of Truth
significance: New Thought spiritual centre in San Francisco
desc: The Home of Truth in San Francisco was one of the key New Thought centres associated with Swami Vivekananda's activities during his 1900 California visit. San Francisco's vibrant spiritual scene embraced his Vedantic teachings, and the Home of Truth network provided organised venues and dedicated audiences for his lectures and classes.

name: California St. Home of Truth
significance: Home of Truth centre on California Street, San Francisco
desc: The California Street Home of Truth in San Francisco was a New Thought centre on one of the city's prominent thoroughfares, associated with Swami Vivekananda's 1900 San Francisco activities. Situated in an affluent neighbourhood, it attracted an educated, spiritually curious audience for his Vedanta classes and lectures.

name: 1719 Turk St. House
significance: Residence associated with Vivekananda's San Francisco stay
desc: The house at 1719 Turk Street in San Francisco was one of the residences associated with Swami Vivekananda's 1900 stay in the city. During his San Francisco period he lived at several addresses, conducting classes and receiving visitors who came to learn about Vedanta and Indian spirituality from this celebrated teacher.

name: Golden Gate Hall
significance: Public lecture hall in San Francisco
desc: Golden Gate Hall in San Francisco was one of the public venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered lectures during his 1900 California visit. The hall's central location in the city ensured a broad cross-section of San Francisco's population could attend his inspiring talks on Vedanta, Raja Yoga, and the spiritual heritage of India.

name: Washington Hall
desc: Washington Hall in San Francisco was a venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1900 California visit. Along with other San Francisco halls and lecture rooms, it served as part of the extensive circuit through which he delivered his teachings on Vedanta to Bay Area audiences during his second and final Western tour.

name: Union Square Hall
significance: Lecture hall near Union Square, San Francisco
desc: Union Square Hall in San Francisco, located near the city's famous public square, was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his 1900 visit. The central, accessible location of Union Square made it an ideal gathering place for the many San Franciscans drawn to his teachings on Vedanta and universal religion.

name: Mount Shasta, CA
significance: Sacred mountain visited by Vivekananda in California
desc: Mount Shasta, the majestic volcanic peak in northern California, was among the natural landmarks visited by Swami Vivekananda during his 1900 California sojourn. Long considered a sacred site by indigenous peoples and spiritual seekers alike, the mountain's awe-inspiring presence deeply resonated with Vivekananda's contemplative spirit.

name: Dr.Milburn Logan's home
desc: The home of Dr. Milburn Logan in San Francisco was one of the residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his 1900 Bay Area visit. Dr. Logan was among the California devotees who provided warm hospitality and support for Vivekananda's Vedanta mission on the West Coast during his second Western tour.

name: Camp Irving
significance: Summer retreat camp in Marin County, California
desc: Camp Irving at Camp Taylor in Marin County, California, was a retreat destination associated with Swami Vivekananda's 1900 California visit. The redwood forests and peaceful natural setting of Marin County provided an ideal environment for meditation and the informal spiritual gatherings that Vivekananda favoured during his time in the San Francisco Bay Area.

name: E.T.Sturdy's house
significance: Home of disciple E. T. Sturdy in England
desc: The house of E. T. Sturdy in Caversham, near Reading, was one of Swami Vivekananda's principal residences during his time in England in 1895-1896. Sturdy, a Sanskrit scholar and devoted disciple, hosted Vivekananda and assisted greatly in his London work, translating texts and organising classes that laid the foundation of Vedanta in England.

name: Prince Hall
desc: Prince Hall in London was one of the venues where Swami Vivekananda delivered public lectures during his 1895-1896 stay in England. His London lectures attracted large, distinguished audiences and played a pivotal role in establishing Vedanta as a recognised spiritual philosophy in the English-speaking world.

name: 80,  Oakley street
desc: 80 Oakley Street in Chelsea, London, was one of the addresses associated with Swami Vivekananda's stay in the English capital during 1895-1896. This Chelsea address placed him in one of London's most cultured and artistic neighbourhoods, where he held classes and met with his many English admirers and disciples.

name: 63 St George's Rd
significance: London address associated with Vivekananda's stay
desc: 63 St George's Road in London was one of the addresses where Swami Vivekananda stayed or conducted Vedanta classes during his 1895-1896 visit to England. His time in London produced a dedicated group of English disciples who would go on to spread his teachings across Britain and Europe.

name: 39 Victoria St.
significance: London address associated with Vivekananda's activities
desc: 39 Victoria Street in Westminster, London, was one of the addresses connected to Swami Vivekananda's work in the English capital during 1895-1896. Located near the heart of official London, it was a base from which he engaged with some of the leading intellectual and spiritual figures of Victorian Britain.

name: 14 Grey Coat Gardens
significance: London address associated with Vivekananda's stay
desc: 14 Grey Coat Gardens in Westminster, London, was among the addresses associated with Swami Vivekananda during his 1895-1896 stay in England. The quiet, residential character of this address near Westminster provided a suitable environment for the study and teaching of Vedanta.

name: Unitarian Church
desc: Swami Vivekananda lectured at a Unitarian Church in London during his 1895-1896 visit to England. As in America, Unitarian congregations in Britain were particularly open to his message of the universality of religion, and these engagements helped build the community of English Vedantists around him.

name: Sesame Club
desc: The Sesame Club in London was among the intellectual and social clubs where Swami Vivekananda was invited to speak during his 1895-1896 stay in England. Such clubs brought together London's educated elite, and Vivekananda's appearances there spread Vedantic ideas among some of Britain's most influential minds.

name: Townhall
desc: The Town Hall in Maidenhead, Berkshire, was a venue where Swami Vivekananda lectured during his time in England in 1895-1896. His lectures at civic venues such as town halls helped broaden his reach beyond London's metropolitan circles to audiences in the English countryside.

name: Pinkneys Green
significance: Village near Maidenhead associated with Vivekananda's English stay
desc: Pinkneys Green, a picturesque village near Maidenhead in Berkshire, is associated with Swami Vivekananda's time in the English countryside during 1895-1896. The peaceful rural setting provided a welcome contrast to the intensity of his London lecture schedule, and he spent time here in rest and contemplation.

name: Airlie Lodge
significance: Residence in Wimbledon where Vivekananda stayed
desc: Airlie Lodge in Wimbledon, south-west London, was a residence where Swami Vivekananda stayed during his 1895-1896 time in England. Wimbledon's pleasant suburban surroundings and proximity to London made it an ideal base for his English activities, and the lodge provided a comfortable retreat between his many engagements.

name: 35 Woodside
significance: Address in Wimbledon associated with Vivekananda
desc: 35 Woodside in Wimbledon was one of the addresses where Swami Vivekananda stayed during his 1895-1896 visit to England. Along with Airlie Lodge nearby, it formed part of the network of residences in south-west London where he rested, received visitors, and conducted informal Vedanta classes for his growing circle of English disciples.

name: Rue Anatole
significance: Address in Brittany where Vivekananda rested
desc: Rue Anatole in Perros-Guirec, a coastal town in Brittany, France, was the address where Swami Vivekananda rested during his stay in this picturesque corner of northern France in 1900. The rugged Breton coastline and peaceful atmosphere of Perros-Guirec offered him much-needed respite during his second Western tour.

name: Mont Blanc
significance: Europe's highest peak, visited by Vivekananda
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited the Mont Blanc massif near Chamonix during his 1900 European travels. The majestic alpine scenery of the highest peak in the Alps deeply impressed him, and his time in the mountains of Chamonix provided both physical refreshment and spiritual inspiration during his second and final Western tour.

name: Mont-Saint-Michel
significance: Historic island abbey visited by Vivekananda in Normandy
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited the iconic Mont-Saint-Michel in Normandy, France, during his 1900 European travels. The ancient Benedictine abbey perched on a tidal island in the English Channel left a deep impression on him, and he marvelled at the devotion and architectural genius that had produced this magnificent monument of medieval Christian spirituality.

name: Matale, Sri Lanka
significance: Town visited during Vivekananda's triumphant return to Ceylon
desc: Matale in central Sri Lanka was one of the towns where Swami Vivekananda was received with great enthusiasm during his triumphant return through Ceylon in 1897. The Buddhist community of Matale, like those throughout the island, welcomed him as a hero who had carried India's spiritual message to the West with spectacular success.

name: Anuradhapuram, Sri Lanka
significance: Ancient Buddhist capital visited by Vivekananda
desc: Anuradhapura (Anuradhapuram), the ancient capital of Sri Lanka with its magnificent Buddhist ruins, was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his 1897 tour of Ceylon. The ancient stupas and sacred Bo tree — said to be a cutting from the tree under which the Buddha attained enlightenment — deeply moved him as a living link to the spiritual heritage shared by Buddhism and Vedanta.

name: Dhaka (Dacca)
significance: Capital of East Bengal, visited by Vivekananda
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited Dhaka (then called Dacca), the principal city of East Bengal, in 1901. He was received with great reverence by the people of Dhaka, who organised a grand reception in his honour. His visit to the city, famous for its historic Nawabi culture and vibrant Hindu-Muslim heritage, was one of the last major journeys of his life.

name: Cologne, Germany
significance: City visited during Vivekananda's European travels
desc: Swami Vivekananda passed through Cologne, Germany, during his European travels in 1896 on his return from his first Western tour. The city's magnificent Gothic cathedral, one of the great monuments of European spiritual architecture, made a deep impression on him as he travelled through Germany on his way back to India.

name: Berlin, Germany
significance: Capital of Germany, visited by Vivekananda
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited Berlin, the capital of the German Empire, during his European travels in 1896. He was keenly interested in German philosophy and science, and his time in Berlin allowed him to engage with the intellectual currents that had made Germany the centre of European academic and philosophical life in the late nineteenth century.

name: Kiel, Germany
significance: City visited during Vivekananda's European travels
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited Kiel on the Baltic coast of Germany during his European travels in 1896. The port city, then home to the Imperial German Navy, offered him a glimpse of the industrial and military power of modern Europe as he made his way back to India after his first triumphant Western tour.

name: Istanbul (Constantinople), Turkey
significance: Historic city visited by Vivekananda on his return journey
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited Istanbul (then known as Constantinople) during his travels in 1900. The city, straddling Europe and Asia, with its magnificent mosques, bazaars, and the grandeur of the former Byzantine and Ottoman empires, fascinated him deeply. He saw in this great city a living symbol of the meeting of East and West that was so central to his own vision of universal civilisation.

name: Vienna, Austria
significance: Capital of Austria visited during European travels
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited Vienna, the imperial capital of Austria-Hungary, during his European travels in 1896. The city of Beethoven, Mozart, and Freud was a centre of European intellectual and artistic life, and Vivekananda was deeply interested in engaging with the cultural and philosophical currents of fin-de-siècle Vienna.

name: Athens, Greeze
significance: Ancient capital of Greece visited by Vivekananda
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited Athens, the cradle of Western civilisation, during his 1900 European and Middle Eastern travels. The Acropolis, the Parthenon, and the philosophical legacy of Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle resonated deeply with him, and he saw in ancient Greek philosophy important parallels with the non-dualistic tradition of Advaita Vedanta.

name: Amsterdam, Holland
significance: Dutch capital visited during European travels
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited Amsterdam in Holland during his 1900 European travels. The prosperous Dutch capital, with its rich tradition of religious tolerance and philosophical inquiry — home to Spinoza's pantheistic philosophy — provided a stimulating environment for his own reflections on the unity of all existence central to Vedanta.

name: Sandukphu
significance: Himalayan peak on the Nepal-India border visited during wandering years
desc: Sandukphu (Sandakphu), the highest peak in the Darjeeling Himalayan region on the Nepal-India border, was among the Himalayan heights visited by Swami Vivekananda during his years of wandering as a parivrajaka. The breathtaking panorama of the four highest peaks in the world — Kanchenjunga, Everest, Lhotse, and Makalu — visible from its summit, deepened his sense of the infinite and the divine.

name: Geneva
significance: Swiss city visited during European travels
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited Geneva during his 1900 European travels. The cosmopolitan Swiss city on the shores of Lac Léman, long a centre of international diplomacy and humanitarian ideals, appealed to his own vision of universal brotherhood. He spent time here resting and meeting with European admirers of his teachings.

name: Zermatt, Switcherland
significance: Alpine resort below the Matterhorn, visited by Vivekananda
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited Zermatt in the Swiss Alps during his 1900 European travels. The dramatic alpine village beneath the Matterhorn offered him the grandeur of the high mountains that always stirred his spiritual sensibility. The Swiss Alps reminded him of the Himalayas of his homeland, and he spent time here in rest and meditation.

name: Saas Fee, Switzerland
significance: Alpine village in Switzerland visited by Vivekananda
desc: Saas-Fee, the 'Pearl of the Alps' in the Swiss canton of Valais, was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his 1900 Swiss sojourn. This high alpine village surrounded by glaciers and peaks over 4,000 metres offered him a place of deep tranquillity and natural splendour during the last major journey of his life.

name: Lucerne, Switzerland
significance: Swiss city visited during European travels
desc: Swami Vivekananda visited Lucerne in central Switzerland during his 1900 European travels. The picturesque city on Lake Lucerne, with its famous Chapel Bridge and panoramic views of the Alps, charmed him greatly. He rested here between his engagements in other European cities during his second and final Western tour.

name: Schaffhausen, Switzerland
significance: Swiss town near Rhine Falls visited by Vivekananda
desc: Schaffhausen in northern Switzerland, famous for the nearby Rhine Falls — the largest waterfall in Europe — was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his 1900 European travels. The thundering power of the Rhine Falls deeply impressed him, and the experience of this natural spectacle resonated with his contemplative spirit.

name: Arabian Sea
significance: Sea crossing on Vivekananda's voyage to America
desc: Swami Vivekananda crossed the Arabian Sea on his historic voyage to America in 1893, and again on his return journeys. The vast expanse of the ocean — its boundless horizon stretching to the meeting of sea and sky — became for him a meditation on the infinite, and he composed poetry and wrote letters during these long sea crossings that reflected his deepening spiritual insights.

name: Aden port
significance: Port of call on Vivekananda's voyage to America
desc: The port of Aden in present-day Yemen was a key stop on Swami Vivekananda's sea voyage to America in 1893. As one of the great maritime crossroads of the world at the mouth of the Red Sea, Aden was the gateway between the Indian Ocean and the routes to Europe and beyond. His ship halted here, giving him his first glimpse of Arabian shores.
